topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the open-plan office hummed a monotonous, irritating tune above Maya’s head. She stared at the spreadsheet, the numbers mocking her with their sheer disparity. Liam, who had been promoted six months ago, was clearly earning double what she did for the exact same workload. A knot tightened in Maya’s stomach, a cold, heavy sensation that made her hands tremble slightly as she tried to focus on the quarterly projections. Every time she glanced toward Liam’s desk, she felt a distinct pressure building behind her eyes, a desperate need to disappear. She picked up her lukewarm coffee, the ceramic mug feeling suddenly impossibly heavy in her grasp, and took a slow, deliberate sip, the bitterness a sharp counterpoint to the dull ache spreading through her chest.

[story 2]

"Just another Tuesday," Sarah muttered, running a hand through her already messy hair. The realization hit her like a physical blow when she saw the salary band listed for the new intern, a junior analyst named Ben, compared side-by-side with her own compensation. A sudden dryness coated her mouth. She started tapping her pen against the desk, a rapid, uneven rhythm that betrayed her internal turmoil. She kept glancing at Ben, who was laughing easily with a senior manager, his confidence radiating outward like heat. Sarah felt a strange paralysis creeping in; her usual proactive energy had evaporated, leaving her feeling sluggish and rooted to the spot, unable to move toward the exit or even pivot to a different task.

[story 3]

Mark sat motionless at his workstation, the glow of his monitor reflecting in his wide, unblinking eyes. The figures on the internal payroll report swam before him, an undeniable reality he couldn't dismiss. He felt a tightness constricting his throat, making it difficult to draw a full breath. He meticulously re-read the performance metrics, searching for some justification, some hidden flaw in his own assessment. When he finally looked up, his gaze snagged on the framed certificate on his shelf—a recent award that felt suddenly hollow. He slowly pushed his chair back, the scraping sound loud in the quiet cubicle farm, and began methodically clearing his desk, his movements stiff and devoid of usual purpose.
